rules.name: That vs. which
rules.summary: Restrictive clauses take 'that' with no comma; 'which' follows a comma and adds nonessential information.

rules.name: Serial comma
rules.summary: In a series of three or more, put a comma before the final 'and' or 'or' — always, in judicial writing.

rules.name: Comma splices
rules.summary: Two independent clauses can't be joined by a bare comma — use a semicolon, a period, or a conjunction.

rules.name: Appositives
rules.summary: Nonrestrictive appositives take paired commas; restrictive ones take none. One comma of the pair missing is an error.

rules.name: Interrupters & attributions
rules.summary: Mid-sentence interrupters ('the Board says,' 'however') need commas on BOTH sides.

rules.name: Introductory elements
rules.summary: A comma follows introductory phrases and clauses; judicial writers vary only for very short adverbs.

rules.name: Dates & places as modifiers
rules.summary: A full date used mid-sentence keeps the comma after the year too: 'a March 20, 2012, email.'

rules.name: Semicolons between clauses
rules.summary: A semicolon joins two related independent clauses; it can't introduce a fragment or a list item mid-sentence.

rules.name: Semicolons in complex lists
rules.summary: When list items contain internal commas, separate the items with semicolons — including before the final 'and.'

rules.name: Colons
rules.summary: A colon follows a complete clause; capitalize after it when a full sentence follows (house style).

rules.name: Em dashes
rules.summary: Em dashes are unspaced, and a mid-sentence interruption needs a dash on BOTH ends — an orphaned dash is an error.

rules.name: Punctuation with quotation marks
rules.summary: Periods and commas go inside closing quotes; colons and semicolons outside; question marks go where the sense puts them.

rules.name: Agreement across interrupters
rules.summary: The verb agrees with the subject, not with nouns in an intervening phrase — 'together with,' 'as well as,' and dash-asides don't pluralize.

rules.name: Neither/nor & either/or
rules.summary: With neither/nor, the verb agrees with the nearer subject; two singulars take a singular verb.

rules.name: Institutional singulars
rules.summary: Courts, agencies, companies, and 'the government' are singular in American legal prose — 'the Board argues,' then 'it.'

rules.name: Indefinite subjects
rules.summary: Each, every, neither, and 'one of the Xs that...' constructions control the verb in ways that intervening plurals obscure.

rules.name: Inverted constructions
rules.summary: In 'there is/are' and other inverted sentences, the verb agrees with the subject that FOLLOWS it.

rules.name: Clear antecedents
rules.summary: Every pronoun needs one unmistakable antecedent; with two courts or parties in play, repeat the noun.

rules.name: Its vs. it's
rules.summary: 'Its' is possessive; 'it's' means 'it is.' Institutional-singular prose is dense with both.

rules.name: Who vs. whom
rules.summary: Case follows the pronoun's role in its own clause — beware interposed phrases like 'the witness who the parties agree was present.'

rules.name: Pronoun–antecedent number
rules.summary: A singular institutional antecedent takes 'it,' not 'they' — 'the company changed its (not their) policy.'

rules.name: Vague this/that/which
rules.summary: A bare 'this' or 'which' pointing at a whole preceding clause invites misreading; give it a noun.

rules.name: Parallel lists & elements
rules.summary: Items in a series must share grammatical form — mixing nouns, gerunds, and clauses breaks the series.

rules.name: Correlative pairs
rules.summary: Not only/but also, either/or, both/and: what follows the first half must mirror what follows the second.

rules.name: Numbered-series punctuation
rules.summary: In '(1) ...; (2) ...; and (3) ...' enumerations, the punctuation and verb forms must stay consistent across items.

rules.name: Dangling modifiers
rules.summary: An introductory participial phrase must modify the subject that follows it — otherwise it dangles.

rules.name: Misplaced modifiers
rules.summary: A modifier attaches to the nearest plausible noun; placement errors quietly change which party did what.

rules.name: Placement of 'only'
rules.summary: 'Only' (and almost, nearly) limits what immediately follows it; moving it moves the meaning.

rules.name: Singular possessives ending in s
rules.summary: Singular nouns ending in s take 's: Congress's, Jones's, witness's — modern judicial house style.

rules.name: Plural & joint possessives
rules.summary: Plurals take a bare apostrophe (plaintiffs'); plural-form names too (Oakes Farms'); joint possession marks only the last owner.

rules.name: Possessive idioms
rules.summary: 'Twenty years' imprisonment,' 'attorney's fees,' 'a week's notice' — measure and idiom possessives clerks must not 'fix.'

rules.name: Compound modifiers
rules.summary: Hyphenate a compound modifier before the noun ('cash-advance facility'); leave it open after ('cash advances').

rules.name: Suspended hyphens
rules.summary: In 'content- and viewpoint-based,' the hanging hyphen after 'content' is required.

rules.name: -ly adverbs
rules.summary: Never hyphenate an -ly adverb to the word it modifies: 'racially motivated decision.'

rules.name: Court vs. court
rules.summary: Capitalize the Supreme Court and your own court ('this Court'); lowercase 'the district court.'

rules.name: State, Government & parties
rules.summary: 'State' capped as a sovereign party, lowercase as an adjective ('state action'); 'the Government' capped as the federal litigant.

rules.name: Numbers style
rules.summary: Spell out through one hundred and write 'percent'; numerals for money, dates, and statutes; never open a sentence with a numeral.

rules.name: Tense in legal narrative
rules.summary: Past for facts and procedure; present for what a statute or opinion says; past for what a prior court did.

rules.name: Quotation fidelity
rules.summary: Never silently 'correct' quoted text — archaic or divergent forms inside quotation marks stay exactly as the source wrote them.

rules.name: Affect / effect
rules.summary: Affect = verb (to influence); effect = noun (a result) — except 'to effect' meaning to bring about.

rules.name: Principal / principle
rules.summary: Principle is only a noun (a rule or tenet); principal is the adjective (main) and the person/sum.

rules.name: Lie / lay
rules.summary: Lie (lay, lain) takes no object; lay (laid, laid) needs one. Even SCOTUS slipped on this.

rules.name: Legal near-twins
rules.summary: Tortious/tortuous, discreet/discrete, flaunt/flout, waive/wave, counsel/council, precede/proceed, perquisite/prerequisite.

rules.name: Imply / infer
rules.summary: Speakers and texts imply; readers and courts infer.

rules.name: Comprise / compose
rules.summary: The whole comprises the parts; the parts compose the whole; 'is comprised of' is the classic misuse.

rules.name: Fewer / less & number / amount
rules.summary: Fewer and number for countables; less and amount for mass quantities (with idiomatic exceptions for time and money).

rules.name: Ensure / insure / assure
rules.summary: Ensure = make certain; insure = indemnify against loss; assure = give confidence to a person.

rules.name: And/But openers & fragments
rules.summary: Judicial prose freely opens sentences with And/But/So and deploys deliberate fragments — neither is an error.

rules.name: Split infinitives
rules.summary: Splitting an infinitive is fine when it reads best; contorting to avoid one is the real error.

rules.name: Terminal prepositions
rules.summary: Ending a sentence with a preposition is standard English; 'up with which I will not put' is the joke, not the rule.

rules.name: Passive voice
rules.summary: The passive is a tool — right when the actor is unknown or beside the point, wrong only as a default.
